audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel / Shemuel Aleph chapter 31, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Gilboa battle, deaths of Saul's sons, Saul's wound and fear of abuse, armor-bearer's refusal, self-inflicted deaths, civilian flight, stripping and beheading, temple display, Beth-shan exposure, Jabesh-gilead recovery, burning, burial, and seven-day fast.

verses.restored: Philistines were fighting against Israel. Men of Israel fled from before Philistines and fell slain on mount Gilboa.
Philistines clung closely to Saul and to his sons. Philistines struck Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi-shua, sons of Saul.
Battle was heavy against Saul. Shooters, men with bow, found him, and he trembled/writhed greatly because of shooters.

Saul said to bearer of his weapons, Draw your sword and pierce me with it, lest these uncircumcised come and pierce me and abuse/make sport of me. But bearer of his weapons was not willing, because he was greatly afraid. Saul took sword and fell upon it.
Bearer of his weapons saw that Saul was dead, and he also fell upon his sword and died with him.
Saul died, and his three sons, bearer of his weapons, and all his men on that day together.

Men of Israel who were across valley and who were across Jordan saw that men of Israel had fled and that Saul and his sons had died. They abandoned cities and fled, and Philistines came and lived in them.
On next day, Philistines came to strip slain, and they found Saul and his three sons fallen on mount Gilboa.
They cut off his head and stripped his weapons. They sent through land of Philistines all around, to announce good news in house of their idols and among people.
They placed his weapons in house of Ashtaroth, and his body they fastened to wall of Beth-shan.
Inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead heard concerning what Philistines had done to Saul.
All valiant men arose and walked all night. They took body of Saul and bodies of his sons from wall of Beth-shan, came to Jabesh, and burned them there.
They took their bones and buried them under tamarisk at Jabesh, and fasted seven days.

translation_notes: Saul's death occurs under battlefield injury, terror of torture, and collapse of his army. It must never be romanticized as noble suicide, prescribed as a response to shame, or used to condemn people who die by suicide.
The armor-bearer's refusal to kill Saul is valid. A subordinate is not obligated to perform assisted death, and his own subsequent death is tragedy, not loyalty's highest form.
The phrase about 'uncircumcised' enemies is ancient ethnic-religious contempt spoken in fear. It may not authorize antisemitism, anti-Philistine hatred, genital shaming, or modern ethnic slurs.
Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi-shua have distinct lives; they should not disappear into Saul's downfall. Jonathan's death particularly closes a relationship of covenant love with David.
Philistine stripping, beheading, display, and temple placement are corpse desecration and war propaganda. They are not harmless celebration or permission to humiliate enemy dead.
Flight from abandoned cities foregrounds civilians displaced by regime collapse and war. Territorial transfer is not a clean military statistic.
Jabesh-gilead's recovery of the bodies repays remembered solidarity from chapter 11. Burial and fasting restore dignity without erasing Saul's harms.
Burning the bodies here is a context-specific mortuary response, not evidence that cremation is shameful or forbidden.
The chapter's ending is lament. Royal failure, military defeat, suicide, and public desecration should never become spectacle.
safety_note: Maximum suicide-misuse safeguards: Do not praise, imitate, spiritualize, or prescribe Saul's or the armor-bearer's deaths. Do not shame survivors, people with suicidal thoughts, or families bereaved by suicide. The narrative depicts injury, fear, coercive honor, and catastrophic loss. Center prevention, care, dignity of the dead, civilian displacement, and communal mourning.
